The auto-enrollment isn't just a one-time event from 2025; going forward, all customers that receive a heat pump incentive should be automatically enrolled in the rate once their Mass Save rebate is fully processed.[^new_heat_pump_incentive_cite]

[^new_heat_pump_incentive_cite]: See [@ngrid_ResidentialHeatPump_2025]

Customers wishing to opt-out of the rate can do so by contacting their utility. Those installing heat pumps without going through Mass Save can manually enroll in the rate by contacting their utility.

**Q.** The cross-subsidy will only be fully eliminated—and these bill and burden impacts realized—if all heat pump customers enroll in the proposed rate. How do you propose this be achieved?

**A.** Through automatic enrollment, mirroring the approach Massachusetts utilities are using to achieve adoption of their own heat pump rate.

For existing heat pump customers, RIE would auto-enroll all customers that have gotten a heat pump incentive through Clean Heat RI, the Home Electrification and Appliance Rebate (HEAR) program administered by RI's Office of Energy Resources, or RIE's own Residential Electric Heating and Cooling Rebate program.

For new heat pump customers, every customer receiving an upfront heat pump incentive through these programs—and through the forthcoming New England Heat Pump Accelerator program—should be automatically enrolled into the heat pump rate.

<br>

**Q.** Why is auto-enrollment into an opt-out rate an appropriate approach?

**A.** Auto-enrolling heat pump customers into an opt-out rate has obvious advantages in terms of eliminating the cross-subsidy: an opt-in rate would take years to achieve the same level of enrollment, if it could be achieved at all.

Such a strategy might be inadvisable, however, if the rate caused adverse bill impacts or bill volatility for some customers.

By design, our rate would cause neither. As proposed, our HP Rate would result in lower operating costs for 100% of heat pump customers in every month.


## VIII. Discussion of Alternative Rate Designs

**Q.** Instead of using a technology-specific rate (based on subclass cost allocation) for heat pump customers, could the cross-subsidy be eliminated by redesigning the existing default rate instead?

**A.** The cross-subsidy is structural: it arises from the interaction between volumetric delivery rates and the fact that heat pump customers consume substantially more electricity than other residential customers, but without having proportionally higher cost-of-service (See Section III).

There are two ways to eliminate this kind of intra-class cross-subsidy.

The first is through **subclass cost allocation**: creating a separate residential rate class with a target revenue equal to the heat pump customers' actual cost-of-service, and designing a rate that recovers that "right-sized" target revenue. This is what we propose.

The second is through **default rate design** alone. The cross-subsidy could indeed be eliminated by redesigning the default rate, but not without a radical redesign.

**Q.** Why would a radical redesign of the default rate be required to eliminate the cross-subsidy?

**A.** The default residential rates (A-16 and A-60) have two components: fixed per-customer charges and flat volumetric charges. Because fixed charges are small, the vast majority of residential delivery revenue is collected through flat volumetric charges. That is what produces the cross-subsidy: heat pump customers consume far more kWh than average — mostly in winter — so flat per-kWh charges collect far more delivery revenue from them than their cost-of-service warrants.

Eliminating the cross-subsidy within this two-component structure requires either increasing the fixed charge (and thereby shifting cost-recovery away from volumetric charges), making the volumetric rate seasonal rather than flat, or some combination of both. But the degree to which these levers must be moved to eliminate the cross-subsidy would produce radically different-looking rates — and, potentially, significant bill shock for RIE's residential customers.

Raising the fixed charge sufficiently removes the cross-subsidy because it reduces the overcollection from high-use customers (including those with heat pumps). But it would also (1) change every customer's bill significantly, (2) be regressive by raising bills for low-use customers, who are disproportionately low-income , and (3) weaken incentives for energy efficiency and distributed generation. In Docket 4770, nearly every intervenor opposed National Grid's proposal to increase the residential customer charge from $5 to $8.50—a 1.7x increase—for exactly these reasons.[^4700_intervenors]

[^4700_intervenors]: TK CITE testimony

But in order to eliminate the cross-subsidy from heat pump to other customers through the customer charge alone, the customer charge would need to increase more than _tenfold_ (10.6x) — from the current `{python} dollar(c.fc_current_customer_charge_monthly)`/month to approximately **`{python} dollar(c.fc_new_customer_charge_monthly)`/month**. (This would allow the total volumetric delivery rate to fall from `{python} cents(c.fc_current_volumetric_delivery_rate)`¢/kWh to roughly `{python} cents(c.fc_new_volumetric_delivery_rate)`¢/kWh.) The full calculation is in Exhibit CLF-JPV-6.

**Q.** Rather than adjusting the fixed rate, could this cross-subsidy be addressed by adding seasonal differentiation to the default rate?

**A.**
Introducing seasonal differentiation to volumetric delivery rates — lower in winter, when heat pump customers consume most of their kWh, and higher in summer[^lrs_note] — is insufficient on its own to eliminate the cross-subsidy. The fixed charge would also need to be raised as to accomplish this purpose.

 The revenue-neutral seasonal rate structure that would come closest to eliminating the cross-subsidy would charge `{python} cents(c.sc_summer_rate, 2)`¢/kWh to all customers in summer and 0¢/kWh in winter, but even that extreme design would leave a residual cross-subsidy of `{python} dollar(c.sc_hp_cross_subsidy_remaining)` per year — `{python} pct(c.sc_xs_frac_of_flat, 1)` of the current overpayment.[^why_tho] (A zero winter delivery rate would obviously be undesirable in practice; and this point is provided to be illustrative.) The full calculation is in Exhibit CLF-JPV-8.

In practice, if the Commission wanted to eliminate the cross-subsidy by redesigning the default rate, I would advise them to employ both levers rather than either one in isolation. I have only done so here to illustrate just how far the current default rate would need to evolve in order to stop overcharging heat pump customers.

[^why_tho]: The reason is that in RI, HP customers consume a larger share of summer kWh than their share of volumetric cost-of-service, largely because heat pumps double as air conditioners, giving HP customers higher summer consumption than average even outside the heating season. No seasonal rebalancing can offset an overcollection that persists in both seasons.

[^lrs_note]: RIE already offers seasonal differentiation in volumetric supply rates: the Last Resort Service (LRS) rate [@rie_LastResortService_2026] is higher in winter than summer.

<br>
**Q.** What about waiting for AMI deployment to conclude, and fixing the cross-subsidy through the introduction of default to time-of-use rates?

**A.** Moving the volumetric component of the default rate to time-of-use would make it much more cost-reflective—by setting prices are correlated with the underlying time-varying cost of delivery. But it _would not eliminate the cross-subsidy_ by itself.

As shown in the preceding question, it is not possible to eliminate the cross-subsidy exclusively through seasonal  rates. It is therefore also not possible to eliminate the cross-subsidy exclusively through seasonal time-of-use rates: adding on- and off-peak differentiation to the seasonal price levels would incentivize load shifting within the day, but it would not solve the structural overcollection problem identified in the preceding question.

It may be possible to eliminate the cross-subsidy through a time-of-use rate by pairing it with a high degree of seasonal differentiation and a higher fixed charge. In other words, such a rate would eliminate the cross-subsidy not due to hourly price differentiation (as desirable as this may be for unlocking demand flexibility), but due to the levers identified in the preceding question—and would be at least as disruptive.

Moreover, even assuming the Commission would entertain gradually transitioning all RIE customers to this rate, waiting until AMI deployment and such a transition are complete would mean accepting many years of continued unfairness and artificially high operating costs for the very heating technology the state seeks to promote.

<br>

**Q.** Why do you favor eliminating the cross-subsidy by introducing a technology-specific rate rather than redesigning the default rate?

**A.** I recommend a tech-specific rate because it eliminates the cross-subsidy directly, simply, and with minimal disruption: the `{python} pct(v.hp_share_of_households)` of residential customers with heat pumps (or as many as can be identified) would be auto-enrolled into a new rate, as is being done in Massachusetts, while delivery rates for non-heat pump customers remaining on the A-16 and A-60 schedules would be adjusted upward slightly—(see bill impact calculations in Section VI).

By contrast, eliminating the cross-subsidy by redesigning the default rate would require a dramatic and controversial overhaul of the rate structure for all of RIE's `{python} comma(int(round(c.rie_rev_req_test_year_customer_count)))` residential customers, as illustrated in the preceding question.

<br>

**Q.** What are the options when it comes to designing a technology-specific rate for heat pump customers?

**A.** There are any number of rate designs that would eliminate the cross-subsidy, as long as they are all calibrated to collect only the allocated cost-of-service for heat pump customers, and no more.

The simplest option would simply adjust the flat volumetric delivery rates down by `{python} pct(c.hp_pct_reduction, 1)`, while keeping fixed charges constant, in order to collect the target revenue for heat pump customers, which stood at `{python} million(c.fc_hp_cos)` during the Test Year. This is what we propose.

The same revenue could be collected by raising the fixed charge from its current level, and lowering the volumetric rate even further.

The other design parameter would be to make the volumetric rate time-varying: a seasonal rate, in which customers are charged more for delivery in summer than in winter, would add a degree of cost-reflectivity to the price of delivery, and would be feasible to implement with RI's current meters. A seasonal time-of-use rate, which could add a further degree of cost-reflectivity by charging more for delivery during peak hours, would need to wait for AMI deployment to conclude.


**Q.** What design does Massachusetts's technology-specific heat pump rate use?

**A.** The three IOUs in Massachusetts currently offer a variant of a seasonal rate, in which summer rates are equal to the current default flat rate in each utility, and winter rates for heat pump customers are lower. In the 2508 docket, the MA Department of Energy Resources has petitioned the MA Department of Public Utilities to order the IOUs the adopt a second version of this seasonal rate, where the winter rate would be lowered even further, in order to eliminate overcollection from heat pump customers.[^ma_2508_cite]

[^ma_2508_cite]: For DOER's petition in the 2508 docket [@doer_Docket2508Petition_2025], [see here](https://www.documentcloud.org/documents/25941015-doerseasonalheatpumpratepetitipetition-requesting-dpu-open-an-investigation-into-a-seasonal-heat-pump-rate/). Switchbox's report analyzing the bill impact of these rates [@murray_HeatPumpRates_2025], is available [here](https://switch.box/mahprates).

@tbl-heat-pump-rates contains the default delivery rates as they stood in July 2025, the reduced winter rate currently offered by MA's utiliities (the "1.0 rate"), and the winter rate proposed by DOER in the 25-08 docket (the "2.0 rate").

::: {.column-page-inset-right}
| Rate Type | Unitil | | National Grid | | Eversource | |
|-----------|---------|---|---------------|---|------------|---|
|           | $/kWh   | discount | $/kWh      | discount | $/kWh    | discount |
| default rate | 0.26493 | — | 0.18091 | — | 0.19122 | — |
| 1.0 rate | 0.20307 | 23.3% | 0.12669 | 30.0% | 0.12094 | 36.8% |
| 2.0 rate | 0.09652 | 63.6% | 0.06652 | 63.2% | 0.0702 | 63.3% |
: Today's default electric delivery rates, along with the 1.0 and 2.0 heat pump delivery rates proposed for each investor-owned utility. {#tbl-heat-pump-rates}
:::

While a lower winter charge introduces a degree of cost-reflectiveness compared to a flat rate, it is important to note that this is not a _fully_ cost-reflective seasonal rate design, where the summer and winter rates are allowed to reflect the actual delivery cost of service for each season. Rather, the summer rate was kept the same as the current default rate, and the winter rate was lowered.

A fully cost-reflective seasonal rate may have produced a summer rate for heat pump customers higher than the flat default rates these customers were on at the time. Such a design would have produced adverse bill impacts and bill volatility for some heat pump customers, which would have prevented the utilities from auto-enrolling all known heat pump customers into an opt-out rate, as described in Section VII.

<br>

**Q.** Why do you propose a flat design for your technology-specific rate rather than these other options?

We reject a higher fixed charge on the grounds that it would cause adverse bill impacts for some heat pump customers, and thus potentially prevent RIE from auto-enrolling all known heat pump customers into an opt-out rate, as described in Section VII.

While we did not evaluate a fully cost-reflective seasonal rate, it is possible that such a rate would also produce adverse bill impacts for some heat pump customers, for the reasons described in the preceding question.

A flat rate would guarantee that all customers would save under the new rate compared to the default, which would clear the way for auto-enrollment, which is essential to actually solving the cross-subsidy problem at scale.

An MA-style seasonal rate, where the summer rate stays equal the current default and only the winter rate is lowered, would also guarantee that all customers would save.

We propose the flat rate over the MA-style seasonal rate because it is simpler: it is a single volumetric charge applied to all heat pump customers in all hours.

We also chose it because it makes the underlying cost allocation visible and verifiable: the rate is simply the heat pump subclass's allocated delivery cost-of-service, minus revenue from fixed charges, divided by the subclass's total yearly kilowatt-hours. By contrast, an MA-style seasonal rate only eliminates the cross-subsidy if the winter rate is set low enough.

That said, if the Commission is interested in tech-specific seasonal rates in order to achieve a degree of cost-reflectiveness, we would support it as long as: 1. the summer and winter rates are set at a level that eliminates the cross-subsidy, 2. the summer rate is not higher than the current default rate, so auto-enrollment is not blocked.


**Q.** Is a technology-specific rate for heat pump customers incompatible with time-of-use rates?

**A.** No. On the contrary, these concepts solve fundamentally different problems, and are complementary.

  - **Time-of-use rates** address **demand management**: they incentivize load reduction during expensive peak hours, or load shifting from such hours to cheaper off-peak hours. They improve *economic efficiency* (see Section IV).
  - **Technology-specific rates** based on cost-of-service allocation address **cross-subsidy**: flat volumetric rates collect more from HP customers than their cost-of-service. Cost-based technology-specific rates solve this, improving *fairness* (see Section II).

 Time-of-use rates alone cannot fully solve the cross-subsidy problem (see preceding question).

 However, a technology-specific rate that eliminates the cross-subsidy can adopt time-of-use pricing to become more cost-reflective, thus improving efficiency as well as fairness.

 While this is a laudable goal, waiting years until AMI deployment is complete and such rates are developed means accepting years of continued overcollection from heat pump customers. The cross-subsidy exists today — it costs `{python} dollar(v.hp_mean_delivery_bat)` per year per HP customer — and the Commission should not defer in correcting it.

 Rather, the Commission should correct the cross-subsidy now through a simple heat pump rate that is viable with RI's current meters, and make time-of-use heat pump rates available as AMI deployment progresses.

<br>

## IX. Methodology: How We Measured the Cross-Subsidy


**Q.** How did you measure the customer count of each residential customer subclass?

**A.** We estimated the share of the Company's electric customers in each heating-system subclass — heat pump, natural gas, electric resistance, oil, and propane — using the 2020 release of the U.S. Energy Information Administration's Residential Energy Consumption Survey (RECS). RECS is the federal government's most comprehensive survey of how American households use energy. It has been conducted periodically since 1978, surveying thousands of homes nationwide on their physical characteristics, installed heating and cooling equipment, fuel types, and energy consumption patterns. The most recent edition, RECS 2020, surveyed over 18,000 households and is the standard data source used by utilities, state energy offices, and federal laboratories for characterizing the residential building stock. We applied the RECS heating-system shares for households in Rhode Island to the Company's Test Year residential customer count of `{python} f"{c.rie_rev_req_test_year_customer_count:,.0f}"` to arrive at the number of customers in each subclass.

**Q.** How did you measure the annual kWh consumption for each customer subclass?

**A.** We used hourly electricity consumption profiles from the 2024.2 release of the ResStock End-Use Load Profiles, a dataset produced and maintained by the National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL), the U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) primary laboratory for energy efficiency and renewable energy research. ResStock models 550,000 dwelling units that are statistically representative of the entire U.S. housing stock, of which `{python} f"{v.n_resstock_bldgs:,}"` fall within the Company's service territory. Each of these modeled dwellings have physical characteristics — square footage, insulation levels, window types, heating and cooling equipment, appliances, and local climate — that reflect the statistical distribution of these attributes across Rhode Island's building stock. These building characteristics drawn from federal survey data including RECS and the U.S. Census.

The energy consumption of each home is then simulated hour-by-hour using EnergyPlus, the DOE's flagship building energy simulation engine. The resulting dataset provides a complete 8,760-hour profile of energy use (including electricity, natural gas, heating oil, and propane) for every modeled dwelling in Rhode Island, broken down by end use — space heating, space cooling, water heating, lighting, and so on. ResStock also specifies each modeled dwelling's HVAC system, allowing us to compute the total annual kWh consumption for each customer subclass.

Each of the `{python} f"{v.n_resstock_bldgs:,}"` modeled Rhode Island dwellings in ResStock represents approximately `{python} f"{c.rie_rev_req_test_year_customer_count / v.n_resstock_bldgs:,.0f}"` of the `{python} f"{c.rie_rev_req_test_year_customer_count:,.0f}"` residential customers the Company assumed in its Test Year.


**Q.** Why is ResStock an accurate source of hourly electricity consumption profiles for the Company's residential customers?

NREL's researchers calibrated and validated ResStock's End-Use Load Profiles against EIA Form 861 utility sales data, utility load research data, and smart meter data from utilities across the country.

The DOE recommends using ResStock load profiles for utility load forecasting, distribution planning, and electrification planning.[^doe_resstock_cite] In fact, ISO-NE's heat pump adoption and winter peak forecasts are built on ResStock.[^iso_ne_resstock_cite]

[^doe_resstock_cite]: See the [p. 5](https://www.documentcloud.org/documents/28021041-doe-end-use-load-profiles-for-the-us-building-stock-practical-guidance-on-accessing-and-using-the-data-2022/#document/p14/a2810710) of DOE's practical guidance on using ResStock [@doe_EndUseLoadProfiles_2022].

[^iso_ne_resstock_cite]: See [p. 65](https://www.documentcloud.org/documents/28021043-iso-ne-final-2025-heat-pump-adoption-forecast-2025/#document/p65/a2810714) of ISO-NE's 2025 Heat Pump Adoption Forecast [@isone_Final2025Heat_2025].

In addition, we performed a weighted sum of the annual kWh consumption of all Rhode Island dwellings in ResStock, which came within `{python} pct(resstock_kwh_gap_pct, 1)` of the Company's Test Year total of `{python} billion(c.rie_rev_req_test_year_residential_kwh)` kWh. We applied a small additional scaling factor to the load profiles to bring the ResStock totals into exact alignment with the Company's Test Year residential kWh, so that our cost allocation is performed on the same total consumption that underlies the Company's revenue requirement.

<br>

**Q.** How did you calculate the revenue paid by each subclass during the Test Year?

**A.** We applied the Test Year fixed and volumetric delivery rates — as detailed in my Exhibit CLF-JPV-7 — to each modeled dwellings's hourly electricity load profile to get its total electric delivery bill during the Test Year. We summed these bills for all households in each subclass to arrive at the total delivery revenue collected from each subclass during the Test Year.

We performed the same calculation using the proposed flat rates for heat pump customers and adjusted default rates for non-heat pump customers.

<br>

**Q.** What methodology did you use to measure the delivery cost-of-service for heat pump and non-heat pump customers?

**A.** We performed a cost-of-service study at the subclass level — the same kind of exercise the Company performs at the class level in its Allocated Cost of Service Study (ACOSS), but applied one level deeper, within the residential class. The goal was to measure what share of the residential delivery revenue each subclass is responsible for, and compare that to the revenue each subclass actually pays under today's rates. The difference is the cross-subsidy.

The steps were analogous to those used in the Company's ACOSS:

  1. **Measure cost-causation.** We need a metric that tells us how much of the system's delivery costs each subclass causes. The Company's ACOSS uses Non-Coincident Peak (NCP) demand as its cost-causation metric at the class level. We use hourly marginal costs as our cost-causation metric at the subclass level — a more granular signal, but serving the same purpose. We multiply each ResStock household's hourly load profile by the marginal cost of delivery in that hour (see subsequent questions for how these hourly costs were constructed) and sum across all 8,760 hours of the year. This annual marginal cost total represents each household's delivery cost-causation "score": the new delivery costs its consumption pattern drove in the Test Year.
  2. **Compute subclass shares.** We sum these cost-causation scores across all households within each subclass — heat pump, natural gas heating, and so on — to get the subclass-level marginal cost totals. Each subclass's share of the total marginal costs caused by all residential customers in the Test Year represents that subclass's share of cost-causation.
  3. **Allocate residential delivery revenue.** We apply each subclass's share of cost-causation to the total residential delivery revenue to arrive at each subclass's allocated cost-of-service. On a technical-level, this is the equi-proportional marginal cost (EPMC) method, the standard reconciliation approach recommended by NARUC for bridging marginal cost analysis and revenue requirement recovery. On a conceptual level, it is the same logic the Company's ACOSS uses to allocate costs across customer classes: measure cost-causation, then divide the revenue requirement proportionally.
  4. **Measure the cross-subsidy.** We sum the delivery bills actually paid by each subclass under today's default rates, and compare that to the subclass's allocated cost of service. A subclass that pays more than its cost of service is being overcharged — it is cross-subsidizing the other subclass. This comparison is known in the academic literature as the Bill Alignment Test.[^bill_alignment_test_cite]

[^bill_alignment_test_cite]: See NREL's Bill Alignment Test paper  [@simeone_BillAlignmentTest_2023], published in _Utilities Policy_ in July 2023.

The methodology is described in full detail in my exhibit CLF-JPV-2.

<br>

**Q.** What cost-causation metric did you use, and where do the inputs come from?

**A.** We used hourly marginal delivery costs — the forward-looking cost of new delivery infrastructure that each hour of consumption drives.

These costs are denominated in $/kW-year, and had two components, both sourced from the Avoided Energy Supply Components (AESC) study:[^aesc_cite]

  | Component | Source of annualized cost | Which hours carry cost |
  |---|---|---|
  | Bulk transmission | AESC 2024 avoided Pool Transmission Facility cost (\$69/kW-year) | Top 100 hours of aggregate New England system load |
  | Sub-transmission and distribution | AESC 2024 avoided distribution capacity cost (\$80.24/kW-year) | Top 100 hours of RIE system load (summer only) |

[^aesc_cite]: See Synapse Energy Economics's *Avoided Energy Supply Components of New England: 2024 Report* [@synapse_AvoidedEnergySupply_2024].

The AESC 2024 study is the standard avoided cost input used by all New England states for energy efficiency cost-effectiveness screening. Rhode Island's PUC benefit-cost framework under Docket 4600 draws directly from the AESC study.

<br>

**Q.** How did you assign these delivery capacity costs to specific hours of the year?

**A.** Using a probability-of-peak approach: the annualized cost of new capacity investment is spread across only the hours most likely to drive that investment. All other hours receive zero delivery capacity cost.

  - For **bulk transmission**, I allocated the AESC's avoided Pool Transmission Facility cost of \$69/kW-year across the top 100 hours of aggregate New England system load — the hours when regional transmission facilities are most stressed.
  - For **sub-transmission and distribution**, I allocated the AESC's avoided distribution capacity cost of \$80.24/kW-year across the top 100 hours of RIE's system load, which serves as a proxy for when local feeders and substations are most stressed.

Ideally, the distribution allocation would have used hourly load profiles for each individual feeder, identifying the specific hours when each _constrained_ feeder approaches its limit. The Company does not compile 8,760-hour load data for all of its feeders, however. [^rie_correspondence] Given these data limitations, the aggregate RIE system load approach served as a reasonable approximation.[^feeder_8760]

[^rie_correspondence]: See my correspondence with Ryan Constable, in exhibit CLF-JPV-4.

[^feeder_8760]: If feeder-level hourly data were available, the high-cost periods would likely be spread across more hours — since different feeders peak at somewhat different times — with lower per-hour costs. The aggregate approach concentrates costs in fewer hours, which likely overstates the cost assigned to any individual peak hour.

Importantly, not every peak hour receives equal weight. Hours outside the top 100 receive zero cost. Within the top 100, hours closer to the peak carry more cost, because they are more likely to be the capacity-binding hour that triggers the need for a new investment. The full technical details of the weighting method are in my Exhibit CLF-JPV-2.

<br>

**Q.** How does the Company perform cost allocation among customer classes in its ACOSS?

**A.** In the cost allocation step of the ACOSS, the Company divides its $2.4 billion in distribution plant among customer classes. The distribution system is broken into functional levels — subtransmission, substations, primary distribution, secondary distribution, and line transformers — and plant at each level is allocated separately using the method that best reflects cost-causation at that level.

For most levels, the allocator is each customer class's Non-Coincident Peak (NCP) demand — the class's annual peak demand, regardless of when other classes or the system as a whole peak — measured at the voltage level where the infrastructure operates.

To illustrate: substations and primary distribution plant together account for roughly
$1.3 billion in plant. The company measures each class's NCP demand at primary voltage, sums these class-level NCPs into a "total NCP" for substations and primary distribution, and allocates the $1.3 billion in plant in proportion to each class's _share_ of this "total NCP". For instance, the residential class accounts for 49.50% of NCP demand at primary in Rate Year 1, so it is allocated 49.50% of the substation and primary distribution plant.[^acoss_cite]

[^acoss_cite]: See p. TK of Book 19 of the Company's testimony and selected schedules.

The same NCP logic applies at other voltage levels, but because demand is measured at a different point in the system, the shares differ. At secondary voltage — which excludes large customers who connect higher up — the residential class's share of 237 million in secondary plant rises to 64.19%, so it is allocated 64.19% of the secondary plant.

Line transformers are the exception among demand-related distribution plant: they are allocated not by peak demand but by a replacement cost study, which assigns 56.73% of $251 million in transformer plant to the residential class.

These demand-related plant costs, combined with customer-related costs (services, meters, etc.) and operating expenses, are summed to arrive at each class's total revenue requirement.

**Q.** What would it look like to use this same cost allocation methodology at the subclass level?

**A.** I would apply the same NCP methodology to each subclass: instead of measuring the residential class's total NCP demand at each voltage level, we would measure the NCP demand of heat pump, natural gas, delivered fuel, and electric resistance customers separately. Each subclass's share of the residential NCP at primary, secondary, and subtransmission would then determine its share of the residential class's revenue requirement (as determined by the Company's ACOSS) at each level.

**Q.** Why did you choose hourly marginal costs as your cost-causation metric for subclass-level cost allocation rather than the Company's NCP demand metric?

**A.** Because the NCP allocator produces inaccurate cost-of-service estimates when applied to residential customer subclasses. It confuses the _magnitude_ of a peak with the _cost_ it causes, which depends on when it occurs.

An NCP allocator measures each subgroup's peak demand magnitude without regard to **when** that peak occurs — and at the subclass level, this produces results that contradict the Company's own engineering data says about when the distribution grid is constrained.

Consider: heat pump customers make up just `{python} f"{ec.hp_pct_of_customers:.0f}"`% of residential customers, yet an NCP allocator would assign them `{python} f"{ec.hp_ncp_share_pct:.0f}"`% of the residential class's demand-related distribution costs. Why? Because their aggregate peak occurs on a cold `{python} f"{ec.hp_ncp_timestamp.strftime('%B')}"` morning, and that winter peak happens to be `{python} f"{ec.hp_ncp_share_pct:.0f}"`% of the sum of all subclass peaks. Meanwhile, natural gas customers — `{python} f"{ec.natgas_pct_of_customers:.0f}"`% of residential customers — would be assigned only `{python} f"{ec.natgas_ncp_share_pct:.0f}"`% of costs, on account of an aggregate peak that falls on an `{python} f"{ec.natgas_ncp_timestamp.strftime('%B')}"` afternoon.

But we know from RIE's own feeder data that the distribution grid has `{python} f"{f.median_headroom_winter:.0f}"`% median winter headroom and zero winter-constrained feeders. The system is nowhere near capacity in winter. Heat pump customers' `{python} f"{ec.hp_ncp_timestamp.strftime('%B')}"` peak is not straining the grid and is not driving the Company to build more infrastructure.

Yet the NCP allocator would say these `{python} f"{ec.hp_pct_of_customers:.0f}"`% of customers are causing nearly `{python} f"{ec.hp_ncp_share_pct:.0f}"`% of distribution costs _because of their winter loads_ — on a grid with abundant winter spare capacity. That is not an accurate measure of cost-causation.

What is needed is a metric that captures _when_ costs actually occur on the transmission and distribution system and measures each subclass's load behavior during those high-cost hours.

Our hourly marginal cost approach does this. It finds that heat pump customers account for `{python} hp_pct_of_total_delivery_cos(c)` of total residential delivery cost-of-service — not the `{python} f"{ec.hp_ncp_share_pct:.0f}"`% that the NCP allocator would assign them.

<br>

**Q.** How did you calculate the total annual energy bills paid by natural gas, delivered fuel, and electric resistance customers before and after switching to heat pumps?

**A.** ResStock provides hourly energy consumption profiles for every fuel — electricity, natural gas, heating oil, and propane — not just electricity. It also provides a counterfactual scenario in which every household is retrofitted with a cold-climate air-source heat pump, with its own set of hourly consumption profiles reflecting the changed fuel mix (more electricity, less or no fossil fuel). We obtained the Company's current residential gas tariffs — Rate 12 for heating customers, Rate 10 for non-heating customers — and monthly residential heating oil and propane prices from the EIA. For each modeled household, we applied these rates to the household's consumption under both its current heating system and after the heat pump retrofit, summed across all fuels, and took the difference as the estimated annual bill change from switching. Full details are in my Exhibit CLF-JPV-2.

<br>

**Q.** How did you calculate the energy burdens of LMI households under their current HVAC systems and default rates, after adopting heat pumps under default rates, and after adopting heat pumps under the proposed HP Rate?

**A.** We divided each household's total annual energy bill by its assigned annual income, using ResStock's income assignments for Rhode Island modeled dwellings, which are sourced from Census data. We computed this ratio under each of the three scenarios — current system on default rates, heat pump on default rates, and heat pump on the proposed HP rate — to show how energy burden changes at each step. Full details are in my Exhibit CLF-JPV-2.

## X. Conclusion

**Q.** Please summarize your recommendations to the Commission.

**A.** I recommend that the Commission:

1. **Create a new residential rate class** for heat pump customers, separate from the default residential class.
2. **Set the rate class distribution revenue requirement** based on a hourly cost-of-service analysis that reflects what heat pump customers actually cost to serve on the delivery system.
3. **Set the revenue target for other delivery costs** based on this same study.
4. **Recover the target revenue for distribution and other delivery costs through a flat volumetric delivery rate**, lower than the current default, calibrated so that heat pump customers' average annual delivery bills equal their delivery cost-of-service.
5. **Auto-enroll existing heat pump customers** into the new rate, and **enroll new heat pump customers** at the point of installation through Clean Heat Rhode Island and the Rhode Island Clean Energy Accelerator.
6. **Recalibrate the rate** in each subsequent rate case to reflect evolving cost-of-service as grid conditions change.

These recommendations are grounded in the cost-causation principles that the Commission adopted in Docket 4600. They are cost-based, and would correct a significant intra-class cross-subsidy. And they happen to advance the state's climate goals — not because the proposal is policy-driven, but because cost-causation and climate policy point in the same direction when rates actually reflect costs.

<br>

**Q.** Does this conclude your direct testimony?

**A.** Yes, it does. And I reserve the right to amend or supplement the testimony as may be needed.

<br>

## Exhibit CLF-JPV-6: Fixed Charge Calculation {.unnumbered}

This exhibit derives the customer charge that would be required to eliminate the heat pump cross-subsidy through default rate design alone — that is, without creating a separate rate schedule and rate for heat pump customers.

### Question

If RIE kept the A-16 and A-60 residential rate classes and adjusted only their monthly customer charge and their total volumetric delivery rate, what customer charge would make the heat pump subclass's delivery revenue equal to its delivery cost-of-service?

### Assumptions

- **Total delivery revenue** is held fixed at `{python} million(c.fc_total_delivery_revenue)`. This is the total delivery revenue that RIE collected from residential customers during the Test Year.[^fc_total_delivery_revenue_note]
- **Total residential consumption** is held fixed at `{python} comma(c.fc_total_kwh, 0)` kWh. This is the total residential consumption that RIE measured during the Test Year.[^fc_total_kwh_note]
- Only two rate components change: the **monthly customer charge** $F$ and the **uniform volumetric delivery rate** $r$.
- The two other per-customer fixed delivery charges — the RE Growth charge ($3.22/month) and the LIHEAP Enhancement charge ($0.79/month) — remain unchanged at `{python} dollar(c.fc_other_fixed_annual_per_customer / 12, 2)`/month per customer.
- The number of customers in each subclass does not change.

[^fc_total_delivery_revenue_note]: TODO cite
[^fc_total_kwh_note]: TODO cite

### Input data

The following table summarizes the residential subclass data used in this calculation. All values are from RIE's Test Year.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import polars as pl
from great_tables import GT, loc, style
from lib.great_tables import get_switchbox_gt_tab_options

_fc_df = pl.DataFrame(c.fc_exhibit_rows)
_fc_df = _fc_df.with_columns(
    pl.col("consumption_gwh")
    .map_elements(lambda v: f"{v:,.1f} GWh", return_dtype=pl.String)
    .alias("consumption_gwh"),
)
_fc_cols = ["n_customers", "consumption_gwh", "cost_of_service", "cross_subsidy"]
_fc_gt = (
    GT(_fc_df, rowname_col="subclass")
    .cols_label(
        n_customers="Customers",
        consumption_gwh="Consumption",
        cost_of_service="Cost of service",
        cross_subsidy="Cross-subsidy",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=["n_customers"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["cost_of_service", "cross_subsidy"], decimals=0)
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .cols_align(align="center", columns=_fc_cols)
    .tab_style(style.text(align="center"), locations=loc.stub())
    .tab_style(style.text(align="center"), locations=loc.column_labels())
    .tab_options(**get_switchbox_gt_tab_options(
        table_layout="auto",
        extra_table_additional_css=[
            ".table-striped > tbody > tr:nth-of-type(odd) > * { --bs-table-striped-bg: transparent !important; }",
        ],
    ))
)
_fc_gt
```

A positive cross-subsidy means the subclass pays more in delivery revenue than its cost-of-service; a negative cross-subsidy means it pays less.

The goal is to find the customer charge $F$ that sets the heat pump cross-subsidy to zero.

### Derivation

**Revenue identity.** Total delivery revenue $R$ is the sum of fixed revenue and volumetric revenue:

$$R = (F \times 12 \times N) + (G \times N) + (r \times Q)$$

where:

- $F$ is the monthly customer charge
- $N$ = `{python} comma(c.fc_total_n_customers, 0)` is the **total number of residential customers**
- $G$ = `{python} dollar(c.fc_other_fixed_annual_per_customer, 2)` is the **annual per-customer revenue** from all other fixed delivery charges
- $r$ is the **volumetric delivery rate** ($/kWh)
- $Q$ = `{python} comma(c.fc_total_kwh, 0)` kWh is **total residential consumption**

**Solving for $r$.** Because $R$ is fixed, increasing $F$ lowers $r$:

$$r = \frac{R - (F \times 12 \times N) - (G \times N)}{Q}$$

**HP subclass revenue.** Under the new rate, heat pump customers' delivery revenue is:

$$\text{Rev}_{\text{HP}} = (F \times 12 \times N_{\text{HP}}) + (G \times N_{\text{HP}}) + (r \times Q_{\text{HP}})$$

where:

- $N_{\text{HP}}$ = `{python} comma(c.fc_hp_n_customers, 0)` is the **number of heat pump customers**
- $Q_{\text{HP}}$ = `{python} comma(c.fc_hp_kwh, 0)` kWh is **heat pump consumption**

**Setting cross-subsidy to zero.** We want

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rev}_{\text{HP}} - \text{COS}_{\text{HP}} &= 0 \\
\text{Rev}_{\text{HP}} &= \text{COS}_{\text{HP}}
\end{aligned}$$

where:

- $\text{Rev}_{\text{HP}}$ is the **heat pump delivery revenue**
- $\text{COS}_{\text{HP}}$ = `{python} dollar(c.fc_hp_cos, 0)` is the **heat pump cost-of-service**

Substituting $r$ from above and letting $s = Q_{\text{HP}} / Q$ denote the HP share of total consumption:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Rev}_{\text{HP}} &= (F \times 12 \times N_{\text{HP}}) + (G \times N_{\text{HP}}) + \frac{R - (F \times 12 \times N) - (G \times N)}{Q} \times Q_{\text{HP}} \\
&= (F \times 12 \times N_{\text{HP}}) + (G \times N_{\text{HP}}) + s \times \bigl(R - (F \times 12 \times N) - (G \times N)\bigr) \\
&= (F \times 12) \times (N_{\text{HP}} - s \times N) + G \times (N_{\text{HP}} - s \times N) + (s \times R)
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $\text{Rev}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COS}_{\text{HP}}$ and solving for $F$:

$$F = \frac{\text{COS}_{\text{HP}} - s \times R - G \times (N_{\text{HP}} - s \times N)}{12 \times (N_{\text{HP}} - s \times N)}$$

The key term is $(N_{\text{HP}} - s \times N)$: heat pump customers are `{python} pct(c.fc_hp_n_customers / c.fc_total_n_customers, 1)` of customers but consume `{python} pct(c.fc_hp_kwh / c.fc_total_kwh, 1)` of total kWh, so $s > N_{\text{HP}} / N$ and $(N_{\text{HP}} - s \times N)$ is **negative**.

In other words: a higher customer charge shifts revenue from volumetric to fixed collection; because HP customers consume disproportionately more kWh, they lose more volumetric revenue than they gain in fixed revenue, reducing their cross-subsidy.

### Calculation

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
_R = c.fc_total_delivery_revenue
_N = c.fc_total_n_customers
_N_hp = c.fc_hp_n_customers
_Q = c.fc_total_kwh
_Q_hp = c.fc_hp_kwh
_G = c.fc_other_fixed_annual_per_customer
_COS_hp = c.fc_hp_cos
_s = _Q_hp / _Q
_denom_term = _N_hp - _s * _N
_F = (_COS_hp - _s * _R - _G * _denom_term) / (12.0 * _denom_term)
_r_new = (_R - _F * 12.0 * _N - _G * _N) / _Q

print(f"""**Step 1: HP share of consumption.**

$$s = \\frac{{Q_{{\\text{{HP}}}}}}{{Q}} = \\frac{{{comma(c.fc_hp_kwh, 0)}\\text{{ kWh}}}}{{{comma(c.fc_total_kwh, 0)}\\text{{ kWh}}}} = {_s:.4f}$$

**Step 2: Denominator term.**

$$N_{{\\text{{HP}}}} - s \\times N = {comma(c.fc_hp_n_customers, 0)} - {_s:.4f} \\times {comma(c.fc_total_n_customers, 0)} = {_denom_term:,.1f}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $F$.**

$$F = \\frac{{\\${c.fc_hp_cos:,.0f} - {_s:.4f} \\times \\${c.fc_total_delivery_revenue:,.0f} - \\${c.fc_other_fixed_annual_per_customer:,.2f} \\times ({_denom_term:,.1f})}}{{12 \\times ({_denom_term:,.1f})}}$$

$$F = \\${_F:,.2f}\\text{{/month}}$$

**Step 4: New volumetric rate.**

$$r = \\frac{{\\${c.fc_total_delivery_revenue:,.0f} - \\${_F:,.2f} \\times 12 \\times {comma(c.fc_total_n_customers, 0)} - \\${c.fc_other_fixed_annual_per_customer:,.2f} \\times {comma(c.fc_total_n_customers, 0)}}}{{{comma(c.fc_total_kwh, 0)}\\text{{ kWh}}}}$$

$$r = {cents(_r_new, 2)}\\text{{¢/kWh}}$$""")
```

### Result

To eliminate the heat pump cross-subsidy through the customer charge alone, the monthly customer charge would need to increase from `{python} dollar(c.fc_current_customer_charge_monthly)`/month to approximately **`{python} dollar(_F, 2)`/month** — more than a tenfold increase — while the total volumetric delivery rate would fall from `{python} cents(c.fc_current_volumetric_delivery_rate, 2)`¢/kWh to approximately **`{python} cents(_r_new, 2)`¢/kWh**.


## Exhibit CLF-JPV-7: Delivery Rates by Line Item — Default, HP, and Adjusted Default Schedules

**Q.** What is the effect of the proposed HP rate — and the revenue-neutral adjustment for non-HP customers — on each individual delivery charge?

**A.** The table below shows every delivery line item, its current rate, and the corresponding rate under the proposed HP schedule and the adjusted default schedule. The HP rate applies a uniform `{python} pct(c.hp_pct_reduction, 1)` reduction to all volumetric charges, while the adjusted default schedule applies a uniform `{python} pct(c.adj_default_pct_increase, 1)` increase. Per-customer fixed charges are unchanged under both schedules.

:::{.column-page-inset-right}
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
print(Path("cache/rate_comparison_line_items.html").read_text())
```
:::


## Exhibit CLF-JPV-8: Seasonal Volumetric Rate Calculation {.unnumbered}

This exhibit derives the seasonal volumetric delivery rates that would be required to eliminate the heat pump cross-subsidy through default rate design alone — that is, by replacing the flat volumetric rate with a winter rate and a summer rate while holding fixed charges constant.

### Question

If RIE kept the A-16 and A-60 residential rate classes, held the monthly customer charge at its current `{python} dollar(c.fc_current_customer_charge_monthly)`/month, and replaced the flat volumetric delivery rate with a winter rate and a summer rate, could the rates be set to make the heat pump subclass's delivery revenue equal to its delivery cost-of-service?

### Assumptions

- **Total delivery revenue** is held fixed at `{python} million(c.fc_total_delivery_revenue)`.
- **Monthly customer charge** is held fixed at its current level of `{python} dollar(c.fc_current_customer_charge_monthly)`/month. Combined with the RE Growth charge (`$3.22`/month) and the LIHEAP Enhancement charge (`$0.79`/month), the total annual fixed revenue per customer is `{python} dollar(c.fc_current_customer_charge_monthly * 12 + c.fc_other_fixed_annual_per_customer, 2)`.
- **Winter** is October–March; **summer** is April–September, matching the seasonal definition in RIE's Last Resort Service (LRS) charge.
- The flat volumetric rate $r$ is replaced by two seasonal rates: a **winter rate** $r_w$ and a **summer rate** $r_s$.
- The number of customers in each subclass does not change.

### Input data

The following table summarizes the seasonal consumption and cost-of-service data used in this calculation.

```{python}
#| echo: false
import polars as pl
from great_tables import GT, loc, style
from lib.great_tables import get_switchbox_gt_tab_options

_sc_df = pl.DataFrame([
    {
        "subclass": "Heat pump",
        "n_customers": c.fc_hp_n_customers,
        "winter_gwh": c.sc_W_hp_gwh,
        "summer_gwh": c.sc_S_hp_gwh,
        "cost_of_service": c.fc_hp_cos,
    },
    {
        "subclass": "All customers",
        "n_customers": c.fc_total_n_customers,
        "winter_gwh": c.sc_W_all_gwh,
        "summer_gwh": c.sc_S_all_gwh,
        "cost_of_service": c.fc_total_delivery_revenue,
    },
])
_sc_df = _sc_df.with_columns(
    pl.col("winter_gwh")
    .map_elements(lambda v: f"{v:,.1f} GWh", return_dtype=pl.String)
    .alias("winter_gwh"),
    pl.col("summer_gwh")
    .map_elements(lambda v: f"{v:,.1f} GWh", return_dtype=pl.String)
    .alias("summer_gwh"),
)
_sc_cols = ["n_customers", "winter_gwh", "summer_gwh", "cost_of_service"]
_sc_gt = (
    GT(_sc_df, rowname_col="subclass")
    .cols_label(
        n_customers="Customers",
        winter_gwh="Winter kWh",
        summer_gwh="Summer kWh",
        cost_of_service="Cost of service",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=["n_customers"], decimals=0, use_seps=True)
    .fmt_currency(columns=["cost_of_service"], decimals=0)
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .cols_align(align="center", columns=_sc_cols)
    .tab_style(style.text(align="center"), locations=loc.stub())
    .tab_style(style.text(align="center"), locations=loc.column_labels())
    .tab_options(**get_switchbox_gt_tab_options(
        table_layout="auto",
        extra_table_additional_css=[
            ".table-striped > tbody > tr:nth-of-type(odd) > * { --bs-table-striped-bg: transparent !important; }",
        ],
    ))
)
_sc_gt
```

For "All customers," the cost-of-service equals the total delivery revenue requirement.

### Derivation

```{python}
#| echo: false
_f_annual = c.fc_current_customer_charge_monthly * 12 + c.fc_other_fixed_annual_per_customer
_F_all = _f_annual * c.fc_total_n_customers
_F_hp = _f_annual * c.fc_hp_n_customers
_V = c.fc_total_delivery_revenue - _F_all
_v_hp = c.fc_hp_cos - _F_hp
```

**Fixed revenue.** Annual fixed delivery revenue is the per-customer annual fixed charge times the number of customers:

$$F_{\text{all}} = f \times N, \qquad F_{\text{HP}} = f \times N_{\text{HP}}$$

where:

- $f$ = `{python} dollar(_f_annual, 2)` is the **annual fixed revenue per customer**
- $N$ = `{python} comma(c.fc_total_n_customers, 0)` is the **total number of residential customers**
- $N_{\text{HP}}$ = `{python} comma(c.fc_hp_n_customers, 0)` is the **number of heat pump customers**
- $F_{\text{all}}$ = `{python} million(_F_all)` is the **total annual fixed revenue**
- $F_{\text{HP}}$ = `{python} dollar(_F_hp, 0)` is the **HP annual fixed revenue**

**Volumetric revenue targets.** Subtracting fixed revenue yields the amount that must be collected through volumetric rates:

$$V = R - F_{\text{all}}, \qquad v_{\text{HP}} = C_{\text{HP}} - F_{\text{HP}}$$

where:

- $R$ = `{python} million(c.fc_total_delivery_revenue)` is the **total delivery revenue**
- $C_{\text{HP}}$ = `{python} dollar(c.fc_hp_cos, 0)` is the **HP cost-of-service**
- $V$ = `{python} million(_V)` is the **total volumetric revenue target**
- $v_{\text{HP}}$ = `{python} dollar(_v_hp, 0)` is the **HP volumetric cost target**

**Revenue identity.** Total volumetric revenue must equal $V$:

$$r_w \cdot W_{\text{all}} + r_s \cdot S_{\text{all}} = V$$

**HP cross-subsidy = 0.** HP customers' volumetric delivery revenue must equal $v_{\text{HP}}$:

$$r_w \cdot W_{\text{HP}} + r_s \cdot S_{\text{HP}} = v_{\text{HP}}$$

where $r_w, r_s$ are the winter and summer volumetric rates, and $W, S$ are the weighted seasonal kWh from the input table.

**2×2 linear system.** Together:

$$\begin{bmatrix} W_{\text{all}} & S_{\text{all}} \\ W_{\text{HP}} & S_{\text{HP}} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} r_w \\ r_s \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} V \\ v_{\text{HP}} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Solution by Cramer's rule:**

$$\Delta = W_{\text{all}} \cdot S_{\text{HP}} - S_{\text{all}} \cdot W_{\text{HP}}$$

$$r_w = \frac{V \cdot S_{\text{HP}} - v_{\text{HP}} \cdot S_{\text{all}}}{\Delta}, \qquad r_s = \frac{W_{\text{all}} \cdot v_{\text{HP}} - W_{\text{HP}} \cdot V}{\Delta}$$

**Feasibility.** A valid rate design requires both $r_w \geq 0$ and $r_s \geq 0$. If either rate is negative, the system is infeasible — no non-negative seasonal rate pair can simultaneously achieve revenue neutrality and zero HP cross-subsidy. In that case, the constrained optimum sets the negative rate to zero and solves for the other from the revenue-neutrality constraint alone.

### Calculation

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
_W_all = c.sc_W_all_gwh * 1e6
_S_all = c.sc_S_all_gwh * 1e6
_W_hp = c.sc_W_hp_gwh * 1e6
_S_hp = c.sc_S_hp_gwh * 1e6

_det = _W_all * _S_hp - _S_all * _W_hp
_rw = (_V * _S_hp - _v_hp * _S_all) / _det
_rs = (_W_all * _v_hp - _W_hp * _V) / _det

print(f"""**Step 1: Determinant.**

$$\\Delta = ({comma(_W_all, 0)}) \\times ({comma(_S_hp, 0)}) - ({comma(_S_all, 0)}) \\times ({comma(_W_hp, 0)}) = {_det:,.0f}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $r_w$ and $r_s$.**

$$r_w = \\frac{{\\${_V:,.0f} \\times {comma(_S_hp, 0)} - \\${_v_hp:,.0f} \\times {comma(_S_all, 0)}}}{{{_det:,.0f}}} = {_rw * 100:,.2f}\\text{{ ¢/kWh}}$$

$$r_s = \\frac{{{comma(_W_all, 0)} \\times \\${_v_hp:,.0f} - {comma(_W_hp, 0)} \\times \\${_V:,.0f}}}{{{_det:,.0f}}} = {_rs * 100:,.2f}\\text{{ ¢/kWh}}$$

**Step 3: Feasibility check.**

$r_w = {_rw * 100:,.2f}$ ¢/kWh is **negative**, so the system is infeasible: there is no non-negative seasonal rate pair that achieves both revenue neutrality and zero HP cross-subsidy. In other words, the system would need to _pay_ HP customers for their winter consumption to make their revenue equal their cost-of-service — which is not a valid rate.

**Step 4: Constrained solution.** Setting $r_w = 0$ and solving from revenue neutrality alone:

$$r_s = \\frac{{V}}{{S_{{\\text{{all}}}}}} = \\frac{{\\${_V:,.0f}}}{{{comma(_S_all, 0)}\\text{{ kWh}}}} = {cents(c.sc_summer_rate, 2)}\\text{{ ¢/kWh}}$$

**Step 5: Remaining HP cross-subsidy.**

$$XS_{{\\text{{HP}}}} = r_s \\cdot S_{{\\text{{HP}}}} + F_{{\\text{{HP}}}} - C_{{\\text{{HP}}}} = \\${c.sc_hp_cross_subsidy_remaining:,.0f}\\text{{/year}}$$""")
```

### Why the system is infeasible

The feasibility of the seasonal solution depends on a simple condition. Define three HP shares:

- $s_s^{{\text{{hp}}}}$ = HP's share of **summer** kWh = `{python} pct(c.sc_s_hp_summer, 2)`
- $c^{{\text{{hp}}}}$ = HP's share of **volumetric cost-of-service** = `{python} pct(c.sc_c_hp, 2)`
- $s_w^{{\text{{hp}}}}$ = HP's share of **winter** kWh = `{python} pct(c.sc_s_hp_winter, 2)`

A feasible solution exists if and only if:

$$s_s^{\text{hp}} \;\leq\; c^{\text{hp}} \;\leq\; s_w^{\text{hp}}$$

In Rhode Island, $s_s^{{\text{{hp}}}}$ = `{python} pct(c.sc_s_hp_summer, 2)` > $c^{{\text{{hp}}}}$ = `{python} pct(c.sc_c_hp, 2)`, so the condition fails. HP buildings consume a larger share of summer kWh than their share of volumetric cost-of-service — most likely because heat pumps double as air conditioners, giving HP buildings higher summer consumption than average even outside the heating season. No seasonal rebalancing can offset an overcollection that persists in both seasons.

### Result

To eliminate the heat pump cross-subsidy through seasonal volumetric rates alone — holding the customer charge fixed at `{python} dollar(c.fc_current_customer_charge_monthly)`/month — the system would need to charge `{python} cents(c.sc_summer_rate, 2)`¢/kWh in summer and 0¢/kWh in winter. Even this extreme design would leave a residual cross-subsidy of **`{python} dollar(c.sc_hp_cross_subsidy_remaining)`/year** — `{python} pct(c.sc_xs_frac_of_flat, 1)` of the current `{python} dollar(c.fc_hp_cross_subsidy)` annual overpayment. The system is mathematically infeasible because HP buildings' share of summer consumption (`{python} pct(c.sc_s_hp_summer, 2)`) already exceeds their share of volumetric cost-of-service (`{python} pct(c.sc_c_hp, 2)`).
